title: Introduction to Functions
blurb: Function notation, domain and range, the vertical line test, and reading graphs qualitatively.

lessons.title: What Is a Function?
lessons.concreteComparison: A vending machine: press B4, get the same granola bar every time. Press it twice, same bar twice. Now imagine a machine where B4 sometimes drops chips and sometimes a soda — you'd call it broken. A **function** is any un-broken machine: every input gets exactly ONE output. Same button, same snack, forever.
lessons.objective: Define functions as one-output-per-input rules, and test relations (sets, tables, mappings) for function-hood.
concept: A **relation** is any pairing of inputs with outputs. A **function** is a relation with a promise: each input maps to EXACTLY ONE output.
Repeated inputs with DIFFERENT outputs break the promise: $\{(1, 2), (1, 3)\}$ is not a function — pressing 1 gives two different snacks.
Repeated OUTPUTS are fine: $\{(1, 2), (5, 2)\}$ is a function. Two buttons may vend the same snack; one button may not vend two.
Functions hide everywhere: each student → their ID number (function); each ID → its student (also a function); each parent → their child (NOT a function if they have two kids).
lessons.keyIdea: Function = every input has exactly one output. Repeated inputs must agree; repeated outputs are harmless.

facts.title: The promise
facts.explanation: Same button, same snack, every time.

facts.title: Outputs may repeat
facts.explanation: Different inputs are allowed to share an output.

workedExamples.solution: Inputs $2, 3, 4$ are all different — each has one output. YES (the repeated output $5$ is fine).
Input $1$ appears twice with different outputs ($4$ and $8$). NO — the promise is broken.

workedExamples.statement: A table maps each city to its country. Is it a function? What about each country to its cities?
workedExamples.solution: City → country: each city has ONE country — function. Country → cities: one country has MANY cities — not a function.

commonMistakes.text: Rejecting a relation because outputs repeat.
commonMistakes.correction: {(1,2), (5,2)} IS a function. The rule polices INPUTS: only a repeated input with different outputs disqualifies.

commonMistakes.text: Judging by the formula's looks instead of the pairing.
commonMistakes.correction: Even a weird rule — 'every input maps to 7' — is a function. Constant machines keep the promise perfectly.

tryIt.solution: Yes — every input distinct, one output each (all happen to be $1$).

tryIt.statement: Each person → their birthday: function or not?
tryIt.solution: Function — everyone has exactly one birthday. (Birthday → people would NOT be: many share a date.)

steps.title: One button, one snack
beats: Input goes in. Output comes out.
A FUNCTION vends the same output for the same input, always.
$\{(1,2), (1,3)\}$: button 1 vends two things — broken.
$\{(1,2), (5,2)\}$: two buttons, same snack — fine.

steps.title: Police the inputs
beats: Scan the input column for repeats.
No repeated inputs → function, guaranteed.
Repeated input? Check its outputs: same → still fine; different → not a function.
Outputs never disqualify anything.

steps.title: Functions you already use
beats: Student → ID number. ✓
Barcode → price. ✓ (Imagine if it weren't!)
Person → phone number: fails if someone owns two phones.
Every lookup table that WORKS is a function.

steps.title: Broken or working?
steps.prompt: Which relation is NOT a function?
steps.explanation: Input $3$ vends both $1$ and $5$ — the broken machine.

examples.prompt: A mapping sends $\{-2, 0, 2\}$ each to its square. List the pairs — function?
A table shows the same store's opening hours: Mon→9, Tue→9, Wed→10, Mon→8. Function?
steps: Not a function — the table contradicts itself.

examples.prompt: The rule 'input any number, output the number of letters when spelled in English' — e.g. 4 → four → 4 letters. Function?
steps: Each number has ONE spelling, hence one letter-count.

steps.title: Patrol duty
steps.intro: Hunt repeated inputs with conflicting outputs.

problems.explanation: Three distinct inputs, one output each. Output repetition is legal.

problems.prompt: A relation pairs each square with its side: $(25, 5), (25, -5)$. Function?
problems.explanation: One input, two different outputs: not a function. (This is why 'square root' is DEFINED as the positive one!)

problems.prompt: Which real-world pairing IS a function?
problems.explanation: A car has exactly one plate; teachers and countries map to many.

steps.title: The word is younger than you'd think
steps.body: 'Function' entered math in a 1673 manuscript of Leibniz, but the modern one-output-per-input definition only settled in the 1830s with Dirichlet — who scandalized colleagues with a 'function' that outputs 1 for rational inputs and 0 for irrational ones. No formula, no graphable curve, just a rule. The vending-machine promise, it turns out, is the ONLY requirement.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A function pairs every input with exactly ONE output.
Repeated inputs must agree; repeated outputs are fine.
Test tables and sets by policing the input column.

lessons.title: Function Notation
lessons.concreteComparison: Instead of 'the machine that doubles and adds one,' mathematicians write $f(x) = 2x + 1$ — and instead of 'feed it 3,' they write $f(3)$. The notation is a NAME TAG plus a FEED SLOT: $f$ names the machine, the parentheses hold what you feed it, and $f(3) = 7$ reports what fell out.
lessons.objective: Read and evaluate function notation, including expressions as inputs, and solve f(x) = k for x.
concept: $f(x) = 2x + 1$ names a machine $f$ whose recipe is 'double, add one.' $f(3)$ means 'the output when the input is 3' — substitute: $f(3) = 2(3) + 1 = 7$.
READ CAREFULLY: $f(3)$ is not multiplication. The parentheses after a function name are a feed slot, not a product.
Inputs can be expressions: $f(a + 2) = 2(a + 2) + 1 = 2a + 5$ — substitute the WHOLE package everywhere $x$ appears.
The reverse question '$f(x) = 15$, find $x$' is an equation: $2x + 1 = 15 \to x = 7$. Evaluating goes input→output; solving goes output→input.
Different letters name different machines: $g(x) = x^2$ and $f(x) = 2x + 1$ coexist, and $f(2) \ne g(2)$.
lessons.keyIdea: $f(3)$ = 'feed 3 to machine f'; substitute the whole input everywhere x appears; f(x) = k runs the machine backwards.

facts.title: Feed slot, not product
facts.explanation: Parentheses after a function name hold the input.

facts.title: Two directions
facts.explanation: f(3) computes an output; f(x) = 15 hunts the input.

commonMistakes.correction: f isn't a number — it's a machine's name. f(3) means 'run the machine on 3', never multiplication.

commonMistakes.text: Substituting an expression input into only the first x.
commonMistakes.correction: $g(x) = x^2 - 3x$ at $x = a+1$: BOTH x's take the package: $(a+1)^2 - 3(a+1)$.

steps.title: The feed slot, live
steps.teach: This is $f(x) = 2x + 1$ as a physical machine: slide the input and watch $f(\text{input})$ update. Evaluating IS this slider, frozen at one spot.

steps.title: Name tag + feed slot
beats: $f(x) = 2x + 1$: machine $f$, recipe $2x + 1$.
Say it 'f of 3', never 'f times 3.'
$g$, $h$, $P$, $C$… any letter can name a machine.

steps.title: Whole input, every slot
beats: $f(a + 2)$: the input is the PACKAGE $a + 2$.
Every $x$ in the recipe takes the whole package.
Parentheses around the package prevent 99% of errors.

examples.prompt: Given $C(m) = 0.25m + 12$ (phone bill for $m$ minutes), find $C(80)$ and interpret.

problems.prompt: $f(x) = 2x + 1$ and $g(x) = x^2$: which is larger, $f(3)$ or $g(3)$?

steps.title: Euler's shorthand conquered the world
steps.body: The notation $f(x)$ debuted in a 1734 paper by Leonhard Euler — a man so prolific that publishers were still printing his backlog decades after his death. His feed-slot shorthand beat rival notations because it names the machine SEPARATELY from the input, letting you talk about $f$ itself. Every programming language's `f(x)` function call is Euler's 290-year-old idea, compiling today.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $f(x) = $ recipe; $f(3) = $ the output at input 3 — never multiplication.
Expression inputs substitute as whole packages, every slot.
$f(x) = k$ is an equation: run the machine backwards.

lessons.title: Domain & Range
lessons.concreteComparison: A pizza place delivers only within $8$ km, and a slice costs \$4 — so an order's total is $4s$ for $s$ slices, but $s$ can only be a positive whole number, and delivery distance only lives in $[0, 8]$. Every function has a guest list of allowed inputs (the **domain**) and a menu of possible outputs (the **range**). Forgetting them is how apps crash.
lessons.objective: Find domains and ranges from ordered pairs, rules, and real contexts, spotting excluded inputs.
concept: The **domain** is the set of allowed inputs; the **range** is the set of outputs the function actually produces.
For listed pairs, just read the columns: domain $=$ all first coordinates, range $=$ all seconds (no repeats needed).
For rules, hunt the forbidden: $f(x) = \frac{1}{x - 4}$ can't take $x = 4$ (division by zero) — the domain is all reals EXCEPT $4$.
Context restricts harder than algebra: 'slices ordered' must be a nonnegative whole number; 'hours worked' can't exceed the week. Real-world domains are trimmed by meaning.
Range follows from domain: for $f(x) = x^2$ over all reals, outputs are $y \ge 0$ — squares never go negative.
lessons.keyIdea: Domain = legal inputs (watch for division by zero and context); range = the outputs actually produced.

facts.title: Read the columns
facts.explanation: For listed pairs, no computation needed.

facts.title: Forbidden inputs
facts.explanation: Division by zero is the Grade 9 domain-killer.

workedExamples.statement: Find the domain and range of $\{(1, 3), (2, 5), (4, 3)\}$.

workedExamples.solution: Denominator dies at $x = 4$: domain is all real numbers except $4$.

workedExamples.statement: A theater seats $250$; tickets cost \$12. Revenue is $R(t) = 12t$. State the realistic domain and range.
workedExamples.solution: Domain: whole numbers $0 \le t \le 250$. Range: $\{0, 12, 24, \ldots, 3000\}$ — up to $12 \times 250 = 3000$ dollars.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting the range as 'everything the formula could make'.
commonMistakes.correction: Range is what the function produces OVER ITS DOMAIN. Cap the tickets at 250 and the revenue caps at \$3,000.
Algebra allows t = 2.5 tickets and t = −3; the box office does not. Real domains obey the story, not just the formula.

tryIt.statement: For $f(x) = x^2 + 2$ over all reals, what is the range?

steps.title: Guest list and menu
beats: **Domain**: inputs the machine accepts.
**Range**: outputs it actually serves.
Pairs: read the two columns.
Rules: hunt what's forbidden.

steps.title: The zero in the basement
beats: $\frac{6}{0}$ — undefined. Bounced at the door.
Domain: all reals, $x \ne 4$.
Set the denominator $= 0$; those solutions are the ban list.

steps.title: Reality trims harder
beats: Tickets: whole, nonnegative, at most the seats.
Hours: $0 \le h \le 168$ per week.
The formula never knows this — YOU do.
Real-world domain = algebra's domain ∩ common sense.

steps.title: Bounce the input
steps.prompt: Which input is NOT in the domain of $f(x) = \frac{x}{2x - 10}$?

examples.prompt: A drone flies at $12$ m/s with a $25$-minute battery: distance $d(t) = 12t$ (t in seconds). Domain and range?
steps: The battery — not algebra — sets the fence.

steps.title: Guest lists
steps.intro: Ban division by zero; obey the story.

problems.prompt: A parking garage charges $G(h) = 3h$ for up to $24$ hours. The realistic domain is…
problems.explanation: Hours parked live between 0 and the garage's daily limit.

steps.title: Crashes are domain errors
steps.body: In 1996 the Ariane 5 rocket exploded 37 seconds after launch: a velocity number exceeded the domain its software expected, overflowed, and the guidance system resigned mid-flight. Cost: about \$370 million. Programmers now speak of 'input validation' — a billion-dollar industry that is, at heart, writing down the domain BEFORE running the function.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Domain = legal inputs; range = produced outputs.
Ban denominator zeros; list set-columns once.
Context (seats, batteries, weeks) trims domains beyond the algebra.

lessons.title: Graphs & the Vertical Line Test
lessons.concreteComparison: A graph is a function's photo album: every point $(x, y)$ says 'input $x$ produced output $y$.' So what does a BROKEN machine look like on camera? Two points stacked vertically — one input, two outputs. Slide a vertical line across any graph: if it ever crosses twice, the machine in the photo is broken. That's the **vertical line test**.
lessons.objective: Read function values from graphs and apply the vertical line test to identify functions.
concept: On a function's graph, $f(3) = 7$ appears as the point $(3, 7)$: inputs run horizontally, outputs vertically.
To read $f(a)$ from a graph: find $a$ on the x-axis, travel vertically to the curve, read the height.
**Vertical line test (VLT):** a graph shows a function exactly when NO vertical line hits it more than once — one input, one output, visually enforced.
Lines (non-vertical), parabolas opening up/down, and V-shapes all pass. Circles and sideways parabolas fail — pick the x where a vertical line slices through twice.
Solving $f(x) = k$ graphically: draw the HORIZONTAL line $y = k$ and read the x's where it crosses — there may be several (that's legal: repeated outputs).
lessons.keyIdea: Graph reading: over to x, up to the curve, out to y. Function ⟺ every vertical line hits at most once.

facts.title: Points are receipts
facts.explanation: The graph is the set of all input-output receipts.

facts.title: VLT
facts.explanation: Two stacked points = one input with two outputs.
facts.latex: vertical line hits twice \u2192 not a function

workedExamples.statement: A graph passes through $(2, 5)$ and $(4, 5)$ but has no other points at $x = 2$ or $x = 4$. What are $f(2)$ and $f(4)$? Does the repetition break anything?
workedExamples.solution: $f(2) = 5$ and $f(4) = 5$. Repeated OUTPUTS are legal — the VLT only forbids stacked points.

workedExamples.statement: A circle of radius $3$ centered at the origin: function or not?
workedExamples.solution: Not a function: the vertical line $x = 0$ hits it at $(0, 3)$ AND $(0, -3)$ — one input, two outputs.

workedExamples.statement: The graph of $f$ crosses the horizontal line $y = 4$ at $x = -1$ and $x = 6$. What does that tell you?
workedExamples.solution: $f(-1) = 4$ and $f(6) = 4$: the equation $f(x) = 4$ has two solutions — perfectly legal.

commonMistakes.text: Applying a HORIZONTAL line test to check function-hood.
commonMistakes.correction: Horizontal lines police repeated outputs, which are legal. Function-hood is the VERTICAL line test only.

commonMistakes.text: Reading f(3) by finding 3 on the y-axis.
commonMistakes.correction: Inputs live on the x-axis. Over to x = 3 FIRST, then up/down to the curve, then out to the height.

tryIt.statement: A vertical line at $x = 2$ crosses a graph at heights $1$ and $6$. Function?
On the graph of $f(x) = x^2$, what points have height $9$?

steps.title: Receipts on display
steps.teach: These points are receipts of the function $f(x) = x^2 - 2$ at $x = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$. Check any of them: over to the x, up (or down) to the point, out to the height.

steps.title: Over, up, out
beats: To read $f(a)$: find $a$ on the x-axis.
Travel vertically to the curve.
Read the height — that's the output.
$(3, 7)$ on the graph IS the statement $f(3) = 7$.

steps.title: Vertical lines as inspectors
beats: Sweep a vertical line across the graph.
Each position asks: how many outputs does THIS input have?
Ever two or more → not a function.
Circles fail; parabolas (up/down) pass; V-shapes pass.

steps.title: Inspect it
steps.prompt: Which graph FAILS the vertical line test?
steps.explanation: Any vertical line through a circle's interior hits it twice — top and bottom.

steps.title: Graphs interrogated
steps.intro: Over-up-out for values; sweep for function-hood.

examples.prompt: From a graph: $f(0) = 3$, $f(2) = 0$, $f(5) = 3$. Solve $f(x) = 3$ and $f(x) = 0$ from this data.
The point $(4, -1)$ is on the graph of $g$. Rewrite as notation, and give one point NOT allowed on the graph.
steps: Any other point at $x = 4$, e.g. $(4, 2)$, is banned.
One input, one output — VLT in action.

examples.prompt: A sideways parabola contains $(4, 2)$ and $(4, -2)$. Verdict?

steps.title: Sweep and read
steps.intro: Inputs horizontal, outputs vertical, inspectors vertical.

problems.prompt: The graph of $f$ contains $(1, 4), (2, 7), (3, 4)$. What is $f(3)$?
Which set of points could NOT lie on one function's graph?
problems.explanation: Stacked at $x = 5$: one input, two outputs.

problems.prompt: The horizontal line $y = 2$ crosses a function's graph three times. This means…
options: the graph is not a function
problems.explanation: Three inputs share the output 2 — legal, and useful for solving.

steps.title: The ECG is a function on purpose
steps.body: A heartbeat monitor draws voltage against time — and it MUST pass the vertical line test, because time can't hold two voltages at once. Engineers designing instruments choose which variable goes on the x-axis precisely so the trace is a function; a spirograph pattern would be useless in an ICU. Choosing axes to force function-hood is real design work.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Graphs are receipts: $(a, b)$ means $f(a) = b$; read over-up-out.
Function ⟺ every vertical line hits at most once.
Horizontal lines solve $f(x) = k$ — multiple crossings are legal.

lessons.title: Linear or Not?
lessons.concreteComparison: Fill a swimming pool with a steady hose: the depth climbs the same amount every minute — a straight-line story. Now fill a cone-shaped glass: the level ROCKETS at the narrow bottom and crawls near the rim. Same hose, different shapes. Constant-rate stories graph as lines; changing-rate stories bend — and you can tell which is which from a table before drawing anything.
lessons.objective: Classify functions as linear or nonlinear from tables (constant first differences), equations, and graphs.
concept: A **linear** function has a constant rate of change: equal input steps always produce equal output steps. Its graph is a straight line, its equation fits $y = mx + b$.
**Table test:** if $x$ steps evenly, compute the output jumps (first differences). All equal → linear. Anything else → nonlinear.
**Equation test:** $x$ appears only to the first power, not inside squares, absolute values, denominators, or exponents. $y = 3x - 5$ ✓; $y = x^2$, $y = |x|$, $y = \frac{2}{x}$, $y = 2^x$ ✗.
Nonlinear tables often confess their type: differences that GROW by a constant hint at squares; outputs that MULTIPLY by a constant hint at exponentials (Grade 10's star).
Rate language settles real stories: 'same amount every minute' → linear; 'slows down as it fills' → nonlinear.
lessons.keyIdea: Linear = constant first differences = straight line = y = mx + b. One failed check fails them all.

facts.title: Table test
facts.explanation: Constant first differences are linearity's fingerprint.

facts.title: Equation test
facts.explanation: Squares, bars, denominators, exponents all bend the graph.
facts.latex: x to the first power only

workedExamples.solution: First: linear ($m = -\frac{1}{2}$). Second: expands to $x^2 + x$ — nonlinear. Third: $x$ in the denominator — nonlinear.

commonMistakes.correction: Increasing isn't the test — CONSTANT JUMPS are. 1, 4, 9, 16 increases but the jumps 3, 5, 7 betray a curve.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to check that x steps evenly first.
commonMistakes.correction: First differences only work over EQUAL x-steps. If x jumps 1, 2, 5… compute rates (Δy/Δx) instead.

tryIt.statement: Table: $x = 0, 2, 4, 6$; $y = 7, 3, -1, -5$. Linear? If so, find the rate.
tryIt.solution: Jumps: $-4, -4, -4$ over steps of $2$ — linear with rate $\frac{-4}{2} = -2$.
Expand: $6x - 2x^2$ — the square makes it nonlinear.

steps.title: Constant jumps
beats: March x by equal steps.
Watch the y-jumps (first differences).
All identical → LINEAR. Rate = jump ÷ step.
$5, 8, 11, 14$: jumps $3, 3, 3$. Line confirmed.

steps.title: Where does x live?
beats: First power only → linear: $y = 3x - 5$. ✓
Disguises exist: $y = x(x+1)$ hides a square.
Expand before judging.

steps.title: What KIND of curve?
beats: Jumps growing by a constant ($3, 5, 7$): a square's signature.
Outputs MULTIPLYING ($2, 6, 18, 54$): exponential — Grade 10's headliner.
V-shaped jumps ($-2, -1, 1, 2$…): absolute value.
The differences are a curve's fingerprints.

steps.title: Read the jumps
steps.explanation: Jumps $-3, -3, -3$: constant and negative — a falling line.

steps.title: Classify with evidence
steps.intro: Jumps for tables, x-ray for equations, rate-language for stories.

examples.prompt: A gym charges \$50 to join plus \$30/month. Linear? Graph shape?
steps: x to the first power: linear.
A straight line starting at $(0, 50)$ climbing 30 per month.
Shrinking — gravity is braking the rise.

problems.explanation: $\frac{x}{3}$ is $\frac{1}{3} \cdot x$ — first power. The others hide a denominator-x and a square.
Jumps of $4$ over x-steps of $2$: rate $= \frac{4}{2} = 2$.

options: a car's stopping distance vs speed (doubling speed quadruples it)
a taxi's fare vs distance at a fixed per-km rate
a salary of \$22 per hour vs hours
problems.explanation: Quadrupling from doubling is a square law; the others are constant-rate.

steps.title: Stopping distance is the deadly square
steps.body: Driving instructors hammer one nonlinear fact: doubling your speed roughly QUADRUPLES your braking distance, because kinetic energy grows with the square of speed. At 30 km/h a car stops in ~6 m; at 60 km/h, ~24 m. Road-safety campaigns are, mathematically, a war against drivers' linear intuition in a quadratic world.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Linear = constant first differences = straight line = $y = mx + b$.
X-ray equations: x must stay at the first power, undisguised.
Growing jumps → squares; constant ratios → exponentials.

lessons.title: Interpreting Graphs
lessons.concreteComparison: A distance-time graph of your walk to school tells a story without a single word: steep at the start (you were late), flat in the middle (you stopped for a friend), gently rising at the end (strolling). Reading graphs as STORIES — where they rise, fall, flatten, and peak — is how scientists skim data and how you'll ace every 'describe the journey' question ever written.
lessons.objective: Describe where functions increase, decrease, and stay constant; identify intercepts and extremes; match graphs to stories.
concept: A graph **increases** where it rises left-to-right, **decreases** where it falls, and is **constant** where it runs flat — always described by the INPUT intervals where the behavior happens.
**Intercepts** anchor the story: the y-intercept is the starting value (input 0); x-intercepts are where the output hits zero.
**Maximums and minimums** are the peaks and valleys — the biggest and smallest outputs, and WHERE they occur.
In a distance-time graph, steepness IS speed: steeper = faster, flat = stopped. In a height-time graph of a ball, the peak is the top of the flight.
Matching stories to graphs: translate each phrase into rise/fall/flat pieces, then check the ORDER of the pieces.
lessons.keyIdea: Read graphs left to right as stories: rises, falls, flats, anchored by intercepts and peaks.

facts.title: Steepness is rate
facts.explanation: On distance-time graphs, slope reads as speed.

workedExamples.statement: A hiker's distance-time graph rises for $t = 0$ to $2$ h, is flat from $2$ to $3$, then rises less steeply from $3$ to $5$. Tell the story.
workedExamples.solution: Hiked for 2 hours, rested an hour, then continued slower until hour 5.

workedExamples.statement: A ball's height is $h(t) = 20t - 5t^2$ (meters). Compute $h(0), h(2), h(4)$ and locate the peak time.
workedExamples.solution: $h(0) = 0$, $h(2) = 20$, $h(4) = 0$: up then down, peak at $t = 2$ (by symmetry between the zeros).

workedExamples.statement: A bank balance graph starts at $y = 120$, falls to cross zero at $t = 8$, and keeps falling. Interpret both intercepts.
workedExamples.solution: y-intercept $120$: the starting balance. x-intercept $t = 8$: the moment the account hits zero — broke by week 8.

commonMistakes.correction: 'Increasing from 3 to 7' should name INPUTS: 'increasing for 1 < x < 4.' Behavior is located by where, not how high.

commonMistakes.text: Reading a flat distance-time segment as constant speed.
commonMistakes.correction: Flat distance means NO distance gained — the walker is STOPPED. Constant speed is a straight but SLOPED segment.

tryIt.statement: A temperature graph falls from midnight to 6 AM, rises until 3 PM, then falls again. When was the day's minimum?
tryIt.solution: At 6 AM — the valley where falling turns to rising.

tryIt.statement: On a distance-time graph, segment A climbs $60$ km in $1$ h; segment B climbs $60$ km in $2$ h. Which is steeper, and what does that mean?
tryIt.solution: A: steeper — $60$ km/h vs $30$ km/h. Steepness is speed.

steps.title: Rise, fall, flat
beats: Read left to right, like text.
Rising = growing. Falling = shrinking. Flat = paused.
Name the INPUT interval for each behavior.
'Increasing for $0 < t < 2$' — where, then what.

steps.title: Intercepts and peaks
beats: y-intercept: the value at input 0 — the story's opening line.
x-intercepts: where the output is zero — sea level crossings.
Max/min: peaks and valleys, with their locations.
Four anchors pin down almost any graph.

steps.title: Slope as pace
beats: Distance-time: steep = fast, flat = stopped.
Height-time: peak = top of flight, zeros = ground.
Balance-time: falling through zero = going broke.
The axes' LABELS decide what steepness means.

steps.title: Read the pause
steps.prompt: On a distance-time graph, a horizontal segment means the traveler is…
steps.explanation: Time passes, distance doesn't change: parked. (Constant speed would be a sloped straight segment.)

steps.title: Graphs as prose
steps.intro: Anchor, then narrate interval by interval.

examples.prompt: A bathtub graph: water rises fast for 3 min, sits constant for 10, then falls to zero in 2. Narrate.
$f$ has y-intercept $4$, increases to a max of $9$ at $x = 2$, decreases through the x-intercept at $x = 5$. Sketch mentally, then solve $f(x) = 0$ and state the range's top.
steps: Starts at $(0, 4)$, peaks at $(2, 9)$, hits zero at $(5, 0)$.

examples.prompt: Match: 'a runner sprints, jogs, then sprints again' to a distance-time shape.
steps: Jog: gentler rise (NOT flat — still moving).

steps.title: Narrate the picture
steps.intro: Anchors first, intervals second, story last.

problems.prompt: A profit graph crosses the x-axis at month $6$ heading upward. Interpretation?
problems.explanation: Below zero = losing money; the crossing is break-even; above = profit.

problems.prompt: A drone's height increases for $0 < t < 4$, is constant for $4 < t < 9$, decreases for $9 < t < 12$. When is it hovering?
Which story matches: flat, then steeply falling, then flat at a lower level?
options: water level as a dam gate opens briefly
a savings account with steady deposits
problems.explanation: Hold, rapid drop, hold — the reservoir sluice story.

steps.title: The graph that spots lies
steps.body: Auditors catch fraud by READING graphs the way you just did: real revenue wiggles, fake revenue is suspiciously smooth. In one famous case, investigators flagged a company because its quarterly earnings graph rose in an almost perfect straight line for years — nature is rarely that linear. The skill of 'this shape tells a story, and the story doesn't add up' begins exactly here.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Narrate graphs left to right: rise, fall, flat — located by input intervals.
Intercepts and extremes are the story's anchors.
Axis labels give slope its meaning: speed, drain rate, profit pace.

practice.statement: Is $\{(4, 2), (5, 2), (4, 7)\}$ a function? Why or why not?
A car rental charges $C(d) = 45d$ for up to $14$ days. State the realistic domain and range.
practice.solution: Domain: whole days $1 \le d \le 14$ (or $0$–$14$); range: multiples of $45$ up to $630$.

practice.statement: Table: $x = 3, 6, 9, 12$; $y = 20, 14, 8, 2$. Linear? Give the rate if so.
practice.solution: Jumps $-6$ over steps of $3$: linear, rate $-2$.

practice.statement: A cyclist's distance-time graph: steep for $0$–$1$ h, flat $1$–$1.5$, gentle $1.5$–$3$. When was she fastest, and what was she doing from $1$ to $1.5$?
practice.solution: Fastest in the first hour (steepest); from $1$ to $1.5$ she was stopped.

testYourself.statement: Which pairing is a function: each book → its page count, or each page count → books with that count?
testYourself.solution: Book → page count (one number per book). The reverse fails: many books share a count.

testYourself.statement: A graph contains $(2, 3)$ and $(2, -3)$. Can it be a function? What test decides?
testYourself.solution: No — the vertical line $x = 2$ hits twice; the vertical line test fails.

testYourself.statement: Table: $x = 1, 2, 3, 4$; $y = 2, 6, 18, 54$. Linear? Describe the actual pattern.
testYourself.solution: Jumps $4, 12, 36$ — not linear. Each output is $3\times$ the last: exponential growth.

testYourself.statement: A drone's height graph: rises $0$–$3$ s to a max of $40$ m, constant $3$–$8$ s, falls to $0$ at $t = 12$. Give the domain, range, and the hover interval.
testYourself.solution: Domain $0 \le t \le 12$; range $0 \le h \le 40$; hovering for $3 \le t \le 8$.
